preFix.title: Fitting a first-flush diverter to a rainwater butt
preFix.subtitle: Improving the quality of harvested roof water by diverting the dirtiest initial flow
preFix.excerpt: The first 20 to 50 litres of rain running off a roof after a dry period carries bird droppings, algae, and particulates from the roof surface. A first-flush diverter chamber collects this initial flow and sends it to waste, allowing only the cleaner water after the initial flush to enter the storage butt. Studies show first-flush diverters reduce bacterial contamination in harvested water by 60 to 90 percent.
preFix.sourceNotes: CIRIA C539 Rainwater and Greywater Use in Buildings 2001. WRAS (Water Regulations Advisory Scheme) guidance on rainwater harvesting for non-potable use. Lancaster University first-flush diverter effectiveness study 2018.

content.text: A standard water butt connected to a downpipe collects everything from the roof, including the first flush after a dry spell. For vegetable beds this matters: the first flush carries high
turbidity
and bacteria. A
first-flush diverter
intercepts the first 20 to 50 litres (based on roof area) and sends it to a soakaway or drain, letting only the cleaner rinse water into storage.
Water Regulations prohibit any physical connection between
rainwater harvesting

systems and the mains supply. If a water butt is linked to a tap or hose that also connects to mains water, a backflow preventer (check valve) is required at the tap. Purpose-made garden tap to hose connections often include this; confirm yours does before filling a watering can from both sources.
What you need

A first-flush diverter kit (50 to 100 mm diameter PVC chamber with a slow-release drain at the base, and a diverter head that fits the downpipe diameter). Most kits include the diverter head, chamber tube, and end cap.
A
downpipe filter
(optional): fitted above the diverter to catch leaves and coarse debris before the diverter chamber.
A 100 mm hole saw for the drill, or a downpipe connector kit, for making the connection to the downpipe.

Fitting a diverter to a high downpipe requires a ladder. Follow the Work at Height Regulations: tie or foot the ladder, do not overreach, and have a second person present. For a downpipe above 3 m, use a scaffold tower rather than a ladder.
Installation

Identify the first flush diverter height on the downpipe. Fit it low enough that the first-flush chamber (typically 300 to 600 mm long) hangs freely, and high enough that the water butt outlet can be positioned below the diverter output. Typically 400 to 600 mm above the butt inlet.
Cut the downpipe at the marked position. Remove a 50 to 80 mm section to make room for the diverter head. Most diverter kits are designed to fit a standard 68 mm or 65 mm round downpipe.

Connect the diverter head into the cut downpipe using the push-fit connectors in the kit. The diverter head has two outlets: one leading down into the first-flush chamber, and one leading sideways to the water butt.

Fit the first-flush chamber tube below the diverter head. Drill a 2 mm hole in the end cap of the chamber to act as a slow-drain: this allows the first-flush water to drain away over 30 to 60 minutes after rain stops, resetting the chamber for the next rainfall event.
Connect the diverter sideways outlet to the water butt inlet. Test by running a hose over the roof or by waiting for rainfall. The chamber should fill first, then water should flow to the butt.
What done well looks like

After a dry week followed by 5 mm of rain, the first-flush chamber fills and the first water to reach the butt is visibly clearer than before the diverter was fitted. The slow-drain hole empties the chamber in 45 minutes after rain stops, ready for the next event. Turbidity measured in the butt water is significantly lower than without the diverter.
What done badly looks like

The slow-drain hole in the chamber end cap was not drilled, so the first-flush chamber remains full after rain. Every subsequent rainfall event diverts all water to the chamber overflow (to waste) because the chamber never resets. The butt fills only from the overflow, which means most rain events deliver no water to storage.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Calculate the first-flush volume correctly: the chamber needs to hold 1 to 2 mm of rainfall per unit of catchment area. For a 20 m2 catchment, 2 mm of rainfall = 40 litres. If the chamber is 50 litres for a 20 m2 roof, it is twice the required size. Reduce the chamber length to 25 litres.
attrs.cause: First-flush chamber is too large relative to the roof area, so it absorbs most of the typical event volume
attrs.symptom: Water butt fills very slowly even after heavy rain

attrs.fix: Clear the 2 mm hole with a thin wire. Fit a small sediment filter (a wad of stainless steel mesh) inside the chamber end cap to prevent future blockage.
attrs.cause: Slow-drain hole is blocked with silt
attrs.symptom: Chamber does not drain between rain events and overflows to the butt along with dirty water

attrs.fix: Fit a leaf filter above the first-flush diverter. The diverter intercepts the dissolved and fine-particulate fraction; a leaf filter upstream intercepts the coarse debris that the diverter cannot handle.
attrs.cause: Leaves and large debris are entering the chamber and being washed through to the butt
attrs.symptom: Water in the butt is still brown and turbid despite the diverter
